Yeah. In part a desire to succeed in this place. So what is, you know, I watched this, there's a lot of dumb things they do, but NSA is also a study in some very smart things, some of it I think done accidentally, but nevertheless. So the National Speakers Association, one of the most amazing things about NSA, it mirrors a multi-level in many respects with this, is they've created a place where there is success unrelated to the real world. And people strive for success just in their place. Okay, and so success there is attendance. Success there is longevity of member. So like if you get an NSA directory and you go through the directory, every member has the year they joined listed next to their name and stuff. Why is that there? Because it's a measure of success. Longevity equals success in that world, like being an auto worker. Their award system. So they have all these awards, a couple with Latin things so you don't even really know what they mean. And none of them are tied to real world. So like certified speaking professionals, fundamentally based on having given X number of paid speeches. You are penalized if you gave any of them to the same client or the same audience. So they have to, I think the number is 100, I don't remember, but it's so it's got to be, if it's 100, it's got to be to 100 different clients. So if you're good enough to get invited back, that doesn't count. And there's no measurement of the money, just has to be paid. So somebody who's getting 50 bucks a speech could be a certified speaking professional. Somebody's getting $50,000 a speech, but only working once a year, you can't be a certified speaking professional. So the award, my point is, it has no connection to real world success. It is a false concoction of success that is far easier to attain than real world success that they all strive for as if it was to the point of being so confused that some of them strive so hard for these awards that they completely screw up their real business, which we don't want to have happen. But my point is, is that there is a desire to succeed and be recognized and ascend in this world they've created that is completely disconnected from the real world. And they've done it brilliantly, and it holds up over years. So that's what that's about. Number six is anxiety and reluctance about disconnecting. Now in some businesses, you guys get to plug in real legitimate pain of disconnect, where utilities get disconnected, we don't really have that pride in themselves, which is why we try and give them opportunities for activity instead of accomplishment. Motivation and optimism. So again, I've alpha coded in this example. Now I don't go through and do this alpha coding every time I do a newsletter, I did this for you. But mostly I get this, I don't now write to the checklist. I used to. I don't write to it. I just write the damn newsletter. But then I do go back and check it against my version of this checklist to see if I really miss something. And every once in a while I catch myself missing something. And or having done something that doesn't serve any of these purposes at all. You know, well, why the hell is this there? Well, because I felt like writing about it. But you know, I'm not writing for myself, I'm writing for these purposes. So I still use it. I just don't write to it like I used to. Yeah. How many pages is you? How many hours? This newsletter is now, I don't know, 50% me probably four to five hours. Well, while the calendar page is a dead page, right? So let's count pages. So so this issue, for examples, a 12 page newsletter, right? So the calendar page is a dead page. Your page is your page. That's two. Yonix page is three. This page is a dead page is four. So okay, it's two thirds me. Right? Four to five. I added a box. Oh, I reacted to it very strongly. Mm hmm. Added it at the last minute. We did a page. You'll see it. Well, having a lot of stuff depends. So stuff accumulates. Okay. Truth be told, it's mostly in one big drawer. Okay. But there's like a couple boxes. So this kind of thing goes in here, this kind of thing goes in here, right? Then there may be a theme. And now I'm actually rummaging for something that matches the theme. There may be one standout item that starts the whole process. Could be something to happen in the news could be something on top of the pile that somebody sent me, you know, whatever. So that's a little random to be honest with you. See, to me now, it doesn't matter where I start. So it used to so I used to write it like in order. Okay. I finish it in a chunk. So by the time I sit down to finish it, there's the opening monologue might be done. And nothing else was or the final word might be done, or the renegade millionaire page might be done because I woke up with an inspiration and ran and did that particular page or something came in and I didn't want to forget what I thought about it. And it was easier to just write the damn peak item than it was to even make notes, you know. So I probably got an hour piecemeal, five minutes here, 10 minutes there, and then three to four hours to actually finish. And I don't finish sequentially anymore. I don't start sequentially and I don't finish sequentially. So one thing may send me back looking for something now to tie to it from the last page to figure out how to start to monologue. So that's more of a random and erratic process, frankly, than you probably want to be in. But at the end of the day, I check it all against my checklist, and I make some final changes. And I do sometimes pull an item out and replace it, change something based upon the checklist. Yeah. But it's harder to attain. I mean, it's fundamentally the same one, winnowed down. And it's harder to hit it, everything, because you only got a page, in some cases, two, every once in a while, I violate it. But fundamentally, I'm working off the same two things. I'm working off of these 31. And I'm working off of this thing. And I'm trying to hit, obviously, not the structural aspects, but the constant element aspects, the objectives aspects, and what do we want them to feel aspects. Now, if I've done one that only was really narrow, it hits like a couple things, then I'll make sure the next one, or the like two in a row, or three at the most, that are like the same thing. And then I'll go do something entirely different. But largely, I'm working off the same list. And in fact, I got the years worth of weekly faxes here. And that's one of the things you're going to do is take a look at them and see how many of these little happy things you can find. For five hours, the writing time, was that also the time you spent collecting material? The collection happens throughout the month. Months, months. It's constantly on the out. Yeah. Yeah. I probably throw out as completely unusable or unvaluable to me three fourths of the stuff that crosses my desk. But one fourth of it accumulates and accumulates and accumulates. Something can get bumped too. So like if I want to do somebody evil, can evil now? See, I can't do it in December, we're past that. So my choices are weekly facts, because I'm only two or three weeks ahead on those. Or one of the other newsletters I haven't done yet for December, which the only thing that left that I haven't done is the NoBS info letter. Or I got to set it aside to use in January. If I don't think I'm going to do that, it's probably going to go into my book pile.
